Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered throughout the semester. I'm someone who learns best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often spend time working through problems physically or practicing concepts rather than just reading. I'd usually carve out maybe two or three longer sessions per week, around 16 hours total, but it wasn't super structured. My anxiety sometimes made it hard to stick to a perfect schedule, so I just worked with what I could manage.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling that week. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline loomed. I have extracurriculars that take up time, so I'd often have to shuffle my study blocks around those commitments. When my anxiety was higher, I'd sometimes avoid studying altogether for a few days, then cram to catch up.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check what assignments were coming up or to watch a lecture video I'd missed. Since my attendance wasn't great—I missed quite a few classes—the LMS was my way of staying somewhat connected to what was happening. Sometimes a classmate would message me about something posted there, and that would prompt me to log in. It wasn't really a daily habit for me though.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a true routine for me. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I'd check in more regularly, especially when we had group discussions happening. The peer support I got from a few classmates helped remind me to check things, which made it feel more natural for a while. But honestly, I never got to the point where logging in felt automatic—it always required a conscious decision.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me because I could pause and replay sections when I needed to really understand something. Since I'm a kinesthetic learner, I'd often watch a video while working through examples on paper simultaneously. I also found the discussion posts helpful occasionally because seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives. Those felt more engaging than just reading static materials.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional resources. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I usually skipped it because I was already stretched thin. Some of the forum discussions felt like busywork to me, so I only posted the minimum required. I think my low motivation made it hard to engage with anything that didn't feel immediately necessary for my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to doing well on exams or assignments. I'm practical that way—if I couldn't see how it would help me perform better, I struggled to justify spending energy on it. Also, anything where I could actively work through problems rather than passively read felt more valuable. My anxiety made me prioritize efficiency, so I focused on high-impact activities.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford, mentally speaking. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them, so even when my motivation was low, I'd push through. Optional stuff just fell to the bottom of my list and usually never got done. There's something about having no deadline that made my brain categorize it as unimportant, even when I knew it might actually help.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then print them out so I could annotate by hand and work through problems on paper. I'm very hands-on, so writing things out and physically manipulating materials helps me learn way better than clicking through screens. I also used my own notebooks extensively and sometimes external YouTube videos to supplement what we covered.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened away from my laptop. I'd take my printed notes to a coffee shop or study room and spend hours working through practice problems by hand. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. Also, I'd often discuss concepts with friends in person or in study groups, which was really valuable but completely invisible to the LMS. My exam score reflects that learning even if my online activity doesn't.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then never need to go back to the LMS for it. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the platform loading slowly. This means my actual engagement with those materials was way higher than my login frequency would suggest. One download could turn into hours of offline study.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety spiked. I'd feel overwhelmed by how much I'd missed from skipping classes, and that made it even harder to log in and face everything. Sometimes competing responsibilities with my extracurriculars made it feel impossible to give the course proper attention. During those times, I'd disengage almost completely for days before forcing myself back.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS felt like this overwhelming thing I didn't want to deal with. Confusion about assignment requirements sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to confront what I didn't understand. My extracurriculars competed for my time and energy, so there were weeks where the course just had to take a backseat. All of this showed up as lower activity online even when I was still trying to keep up.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out completely. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other things or just too mentally exhausted to absorb anything. I'd technically be logged in and watching something, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually engaging with the material meaningfully.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. My anxiety made starting things feel really daunting, so I'd avoid them until the pressure became unbearable. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same avoidance. It's frustrating because I knew what I needed to do, but the emotional barrier felt insurmountable in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing that got me to engage with the LMS. Without a deadline approaching, I had very little urgency to log in or complete anything. I'd often have bursts of activity right before something was due, which probably looked erratic from a data perspective. The structure of having clear due dates helped me function despite my low motivation—without them, I probably would have done even less.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it actually boosted my confidence and made me more willing to engage with the next assignment. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and feel disconnected from the course. The peer support I received helped more than formal feedback sometimes—knowing classmates were rooting for me made a difference. My high exam score gave me confidence that my methods were working, even if unconventional.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging for me. Having six online courses meant a lot of content to manage, which sometimes felt overwhelming. The flexibility of online materials was great for my irregular schedule, but it also meant I could easily fall behind without noticing. I needed more external structure than the course provided, which is partly why peer support became so important for me.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier for my learning style. However, the discussion requirements felt forced and didn't really match how I naturally learn. When activities required active participation online, I struggled more than with self-paced materials. I think having more hands-on or practical components would have suited me better than the text-heavy format of some modules.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for what they need without finding it, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. Sometimes high activity is just anxiety manifesting as constant checking rather than productive engagement. They might also be confused about requirements and spending time trying to figure things out. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying—they might just be struggling differently.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My exam score was nearly perfect, but my LMS activity was relatively low because I did most of my learning offline. Once I understood the material through my own methods—printing things out, working problems by hand, studying with peers—I didn't need to keep going back to the platform. Low activity doesn't mean low learning; it might just mean different learning strategies.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show when someone accesses materials and how often they log in, which gives a rough picture of online engagement patterns. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during a session, emotional state, and learning that happens through peer discussions or external resources. They show behavior but not understanding, presence but not depth. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. For me, it's when I'm actively wrestling with concepts, asking questions, and connecting ideas to things I already know. It's a mental state more than a set of behaviors—I can be deeply engaged while sitting quietly with printed notes, or completely disengaged while technically watching a video online.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to stay motivated, or how my anxiety affected my patterns of engagement. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from printed materials or the meaningful conversations with classmates that helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show that despite my irregular online presence, I was actually deeply committed to understanding the material—my exam score proves that. The human side of learning is invisible to these systems.
